source.title: Accelerated enzyme engineering by machine-learning guided cell-free expression

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Enzyme engineering is limited by the challenge of rapidly generating and using large datasets of sequence-function relationships for predictive design. Traditional directed evolution approaches are low throughput, constrain resampling of mutations, miss epistatic interactions, and focus on single transformations, limiting data collection for forward engineering. The goal is to develop a high-throughput, ML-guided platform that integrates cell-free DNA assembly, cell-free gene expression, and functional assays to rapidly map fitness landscapes across protein sequence space and optimize enzymes for multiple, distinct chemical reactions, reducing screening burden.

section_gists.heading: Accelerated enzyme engineering by machine-learning guided cell-free expression
section_gists.gist: The paper introduces a machine learning (ML)-guided platform that integrates cell-free DNA assembly, cell-free gene expression, and functional assays to rapidly map fitness landscapes and optimize enzymes for multiple chemical reactions. The platform is applied to engineer amide synthetases by evaluating substrate preference for 1217 enzyme variants in 10,953 unique reactions. Augmented ridge regression ML models are built to predict variants for 9 small molecule pharmaceuticals, achieving 1.6- to 42-fold improved activity. The framework enables iterative exploration of protein sequence space to build specialized biocatalysts in parallel.
process_signals: Enzyme engineering is limited by challenge of rapidly generating and using large datasets of sequence-function relationships.
Current directed evolution approaches are low throughput, constrain resampling mutations, miss epistatic interactions, and focus on single transformations.
ML models have been used but rapidly building datasets to navigate vast sequence space remains a challenge.
The platform uses cell-free gene expression to allow rapid synthesis and functional testing of proteins in a design-build-test-learn workflow.
The framework first maps sequence-function relationships for single-order mutations, then uses data to fit supervised ridge regression ML models augmented with zero-shot predictions.
metrics: 1.6- to 42-fold improved activity relative to parent
1217 enzyme variants evaluated in 10,953 unique reactions

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Exploring the biocatalytic synthesis landscape of McbA
section_gists.gist: The goal was to develop an ML-guided DBTL workflow to expedite simultaneous directed evolution campaigns by reducing screening burden. To identify reactions of interest, the amidation reaction space of wild-type McbA was explored by evaluating enzymatic substrate promiscuity with 1100 unique reactions. McbA showed tolerance to various functional groups but had limitations: aliphatic acids were poorly tolerated, aryl amines were difficult, and some important molecules like nonivamide, capsaicin, imatinib, nilotinib were not synthesized. McbA synthesized 11 pharmaceutical compounds and showed stereoselectivity and chemoselectivity. Inaccessible products suggest future engineering work.
process_signals: Goal: develop ML-guided DBTL workflow to expedite simultaneous directed evolution campaigns by reducing screening burden.
First step: explore amidation reaction space of wild-type McbA by evaluating enzymatic substrate promiscuity.
1100 unique reactions carried out with low enzyme concentration and high substrate concentration.
McbA displayed tolerance to multiple unprotected functional groups but had clear limitations: aliphatic acids poorly tolerated, aryl amines difficult.
Several important molecules not synthesized by wild-type McbA, guiding future engineering work.
McbA synthesized 11 pharmaceutical compounds, showing stereoselectivity and chemoselectivity.
methods: Substrate promiscuity evaluation with 1100 unique reactions
Low enzyme concentration (~1 µM) and high substrate concentration (25 mM)
Detection by UV absorbance and mass spectrometry
failures_or_limits: Nonivamide, capsaicin, imatinib, nilotinib not synthesized
Some products only trace amounts detectable by MS

section_gists.heading: Cell-free protein engineering to rapidly screen sequence-defined protein libraries
section_gists.gist: With specific chemical transformations identified, the next step was to quickly generate sequence-function relationship data of mutant McbA enzymes for training ML models. A cell-free protein synthesis approach was implemented that avoids laborious transformation and cloning steps. The workflow uses cell-free DNA assembly and CFE to build site-saturated, sequence-defined protein libraries in five steps. The method was validated using muGFP, showing high tolerance to primer design deviations and correct mutations. Applied to McbA for three high-value molecules: moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine, chosen based on substrate scope evaluation. Engineering campaigns were run in parallel to infer mutations influencing substrate specificity.
process_signals: Goal: quickly generate large amounts of sequence-function relationship data of mutant McbA enzymes for training ML models.
Implemented cell-free protein synthesis approach to avoid laborious transformation and cloning steps.
Workflow: DNA primer with mismatch introduces mutation via PCR, DpnI digest, intramolecular Gibson assembly, second PCR amplifies linear DNA expression templates, protein expressed through CFE.
Validated using muGFP, targeting four residues important for stability and fluorescence.

Applied to McbA for three high-value molecules: moclobemide (high promiscuity, 12% wt conversion), metoclopramide (unique challenge with free amine, 3% wt conversion), cinchocaine (unique acid, 2% wt conversion).
Engineering campaigns run in parallel to infer mutations influencing substrate specificity.
methods: PCR with mismatch primers, DpnI digest, Gibson assembly

section_gists.heading: ML-guided, cell-free expression for protein engineering
section_gists.gist:
With single mutant data from hot spot screens (HSS), ML models were used to predict higher order mutants with increased activity. Augmented ridge regression models were chosen for simplicity and prior performance. Variant features: site-specific amino acid encodings concatenated with zero-shot fitness predictions. Tested encodings (one-hot, Georgiev, etc.) and zero-shot predictors (ESM-1b, EVmutation, MAESTRO). Training on single mutant data (n=77) from HSS. In parallel, traditional ISM campaigns were run for moclobemide, metoclopramide, cinchocaine. Moclobemide ISM yielded quadruple mutant with 96% conversion (42-fold increase in catalytic efficiency). Metoclopramide ISM yielded quadruple mutant with 30-fold improvement. Cinchocaine ISM failed to find beneficial mutations beyond double mutant, supporting need for ML. Model evaluation used NDCG and Spearman; augmented models outperformed ridge alone. Combining predictors did not improve performance. Using full site saturation dataset (
...[clipped]...
process_signals: Key idea: use single mutant data from HSS to extrapolate higher order mutants with increased activity.
Chose augmented ridge regression models for simplicity and prior performance.
Variant features: site-specific amino acid encodings concatenated with zero-shot fitness prediction.
Tested multiple amino acid encodings and zero-shot predictors (ESM-1b, EVmutation, MAESTRO).
Training on single mutant data (n=77) from HSS (top four hot spots).
In parallel, conducted traditional ISM campaigns for benchmarking.
Moclobemide ISM: selected 6 residues, 3 rounds, quadruple mutant with 96% conversion, 42-fold increase in catalytic efficiency, no significant change in melting point.
Metoclopramide ISM: selected 10 residues, 3 rounds, quadruple mutant with 30-fold improvement.
Cinchocaine ISM: failed to observe beneficial mutations beyond double mutant, supporting need for ML to capture epistatic interactions.
Model evaluation: NDCG and Spearman; augmented models outperformed ridge alone; combining predictors did not improve performance.
Full site saturation dataset (n=77) gave more predictive power than reduced libraries (NDT, NRT, single amino acid scans, BLOSUM).
Final model: EVmutation augmented with Georgiev encodings.
Top 25 predictions tested; ML-predicted variants outperformed best rational design (combining best single mutants).
methods: Site-specific amino acid encodings (Georgiev, one-hot, etc.)
Hot spot screen (HSS) to select residues
Normalized discounted cumulative gain (NDCG) for model evaluation
metrics: Moclobemide: 96% conversion, 42-fold increase in catalytic efficiency (kcat/KM from 18.2 to 769 M-1 min-1)
Cinchocaine: no beneficial mutations beyond double mutant
Model performance: NDCG and Spearman showed augmented models better
Full dataset (n=77) more predictive than reduced libraries
failures_or_limits: Cinchocaine ISM failed to find beneficial mutations beyond double mutant, indicating dead ends in traditional directed evolution.
Combining zero-shot predictors did not improve model performance.
Reduced libraries (NDT, NRT, single amino acid scans, BLOSUM) gave lower predictive power than full site saturation dataset.

section_gists.heading: ML-guided biocatalytic diversification for high-value pharmaceuticals
section_gists.gist: To assess robustness, the ML-guided framework was applied to predict McbA mutants for six additional pharmaceutical compounds. For each, the same instance of the 1216 single mutant library was used for HSS (7302 reactions), four hot spots selected, ML model trained, top 24 predictions tested. Best variant compared to wt-McbA showed 1.6- to 34-fold improvement. Best predicted mutant always outperformed best rational design. Some modest improvements due to low signal-to-noise in HSS for trace products. Example: troxipide, wt-McbA proficient at adenylation but unable to catalyze amide bond formation; engineered variant accepted amine, reducing intermediate. Engineered variants for each target product were specific.
process_signals: Applied ML-guided framework to six additional pharmaceutical compounds to assess robustness.
Used same 1216 single mutant library for HSS (7302 reactions total), selected four hot spots, trained ML model, tested top 24 predictions.
Best variant compared to wt-McbA: 1.6- to 34-fold improvement.
Best predicted mutant always outperformed best rational design (combining four best mutations from HSS).
Some modest improvements attributed to low signal-to-noise in HSS for trace products, leading to flat fitness landscapes.
Example: troxipide, wt-McbA proficient at adenylation but unable to catalyze amide bond formation; engineered variant accepted amine, reducing intermediate.
Engineered variants were specific to each target product.
methods: Hot spot screen (HSS) using 1216 single mutant library
ML model training and prediction (same as before)
Comparison of ML-predicted variants vs. rational design
metrics: 1.6- to 34-fold improvement over wt-McbA for six compounds
failures_or_limits: Some compounds had modest improvements due to low signal-to-noise in HSS, resulting in flat fitness landscapes difficult to model.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist:
The work established a high-throughput, ML-guided protein engineering framework integrating CFE and mutagenesis, ML to expedite directed evolution, and divergent evolution to convert a generalist enzyme into multiple specialists. Explored 2856 McbA variants, 1100 possible amide products, 12,584 substrate pair-mutant reactions. Identified 19 unique residue positions impacting biocatalysis; each reaction yielded unique hot spots. ML models trained on single-residue mutations predicted higher order mutants with improved fitness; augmented ridge regression performed well. In all nine test cases, ML-predicted 4-mutation variants had greater activity than combination of four best single mutants. Approach can be applied to any enzyme but requires reaction-specific fine-tuning. Screening remains a bottleneck; LC-MS provided manageable solution. Alternative ML models may be needed for complex fitness landscapes. Future improvements: training on more residues, multi-mutant data, kinetic measurem
...[clipped]...
process_signals: Framework integrated CFE and mutagenesis, ML to expedite directed evolution, divergent evolution to convert generalist into specialists.
Explored 2856 McbA variants, 1100 possible amide products, 12,584 substrate pair-mutant reactions.
Identified 19 unique residue positions impacting biocatalysis; each reaction yielded unique hot spots.
ML models trained on single-residue mutations predicted higher order mutants with improved fitness; augmented ridge regression performed well.
In all nine test cases, ML-predicted 4-mutation variants had greater activity than combination of four best single mutants.
Observation holds even with flat fitness landscapes or high signal-to-noise, highlighting robustness.
Approach can be applied to any enzyme but requires reaction-specific fine-tuning around data collection and ML model generation.
Screening remains a bottleneck; LC-MS provided manageable solution (~3 min/sample).
Alternative ML models (random forests, SVMs, neural networks) may be needed for complex fitness landscapes.
Future improvements: training on more residues, multi-mutant data, kinetic measurements.
Framework overcomes path dependencies, speeds engineering relative to ISM alone; six campaigns completed in 1 week per compound.
Low cost (cents per 10-µL reaction) and high scalability of CFE.
metrics: Six campaigns completed in 1 week per compound
failures_or_limits: Screening remains a bottleneck; LC-MS manageable but may not suit all enzymes.
More complex ML models may be needed for complex fitness landscapes.
Training on more residues, multi-mutant data, or kinetic measurements could improve predictions.
Residue selection (hot spots) more related to overall reaction than individual fragments, making rationalization difficult.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: This is a section heading only, no content.

section_gists.heading: Machine learning-guided directed evolution
section_gists.gist:
Ridge regression models were augmented following Hsu et al. Variant featurization: concatenate zero-shot predictions with site-specific amino acid encodings. Zero-shot predictions standardized and regularized (regularization strength 10^-8). L2 regularization strength (α) determined by hyperparameter tuning via cross-validation. Model evaluation and selection performed retrospectively using moclobemide and metoclopramide data. Augmented models trained on single site saturation libraries for four residues (n≈80) and tested on withheld higher-order mutants (n≈200). Hot spots determined by selecting four residues with highest improvement in yield among 64 tested. Hyperparameter tuning: repeated 5-fold cross-validation (20 repeats), 80% training, 20% test, scored by MSE. Optimized hyperparameter used to predict on test set; Spearman and NDCG used to select best zero-shot predictor and encoding (preference to NDCG). Best model: EVmutation probability density model with Georgiev encodings. P
...[clipped]...
process_signals: Ridge regression models augmented following Hsu et al. code.
Variant featurization: concatenate zero-shot predictions with site-specific amino acid encodings.
Zero-shot predictions standardized and regularized (regularization strength 10^-8).
L2 regularization strength (α) determined by hyperparameter tuning via cross-validation.
Model evaluation and selection performed retrospectively using moclobemide and metoclopramide data.
Augmented models trained on single site saturation libraries for four residues (n≈80) and tested on withheld higher-order mutants (n≈200).
Hot spots determined by selecting four residues with highest improvement in yield among 64 tested.
Hyperparameter tuning: repeated 5-fold cross-validation (20 repeats), 80% training, 20% test, scored by MSE.
Optimized hyperparameter used to predict on test set; Spearman and NDCG used to select best zero-shot predictor and encoding (preference to NDCG).
Best model: EVmutation probability density model with Georgiev encodings.
Predictions made on entire combinatorial dataset (n=160,000); top 25 tested.
For remaining seven amide products, similar process.
Model training and predictions took minutes on standard hardware (12th Gen Intel Core i7, 32 GB RAM, no GPU).
methods: Site-specific amino acid encodings (Georgiev, one-hot, etc.)
Model evaluation with NDCG and Spearman correlation
failures_or_limits: Minor differences in predictions ranked 23-25 for metoclopramide and moclobemide due to changes between initial model development and reimplementation.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The initial problem is that traditional directed evolution is limited by low throughput and inability to capture epistatic interactions, hindering rapid enzyme engineering.

The hypothesis is that integrating cell-free expression with ML can accelerate enzyme engineering by rapidly generating sequence-function data and predicting higher-order mutants from single mutant data.
The approach first explores substrate promiscuity of McbA to identify target reactions, then uses cell-free methods to build and screen site-saturated libraries for multiple reactions in parallel.
ML models (augmented ridge regression) are trained on single mutant data to predict higher-order mutants, validated against traditional ISM campaigns.
The framework is applied to nine pharmaceutical compounds, demonstrating improved activity and generalization.
Limitations include screening bottlenecks, need for reaction-specific fine-tuning, and potential need for more complex ML models for complex landscapes.
uncertainties_to_resolve: How generalizable is the framework to other enzyme classes beyond amide synthetases?
Can the approach be scaled to larger sequence spaces (more residues, more mutations) without exponential increase in screening?
Would more complex ML models (e.g., neural networks) significantly improve predictions for difficult fitness landscapes?
What is the minimal dataset size required for effective ML predictions in different enzyme systems?
How does the choice of zero-shot predictor depend on the enzyme and reaction type?
Can the framework incorporate kinetic parameters or multi-mutant data to improve predictions?
What are the practical limits of cell-free expression for enzymes requiring post-translational modifications or cofactors?

events.summary: Enzyme engineering is limited by the challenge of rapidly generating and using large datasets of sequence-function relationships for predictive design.
Current directed evolution approaches are low throughput, constrain resampling mutations, miss epistatic interactions, and focus on single transformations.

A high-throughput, ML-guided platform integrating cell-free DNA assembly, cell-free gene expression, and functional assays can rapidly map fitness landscapes and optimize enzymes for multiple reactions.
Applied platform to engineer amide synthetases by evaluating substrate preference for 1217 enzyme variants in 10,953 unique reactions.
ML-predicted enzyme variants demonstrate 1.6- to 42-fold improved activity relative to the parent over nine compounds.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk introduces the core problem, hypothesis, design, initial experimental application, validation results, and a limitation, all explicitly stated.

events.summary: The cinchocaine ISM campaign failed to find beneficial mutations beyond a double mutant, motivating the use of ML models to capture epistatic interactions.
The key idea was to use single mutant data from HSS to extrapolate higher order mutants with increased activity using augmented ridge regression models.
Variant feature representations consisted of site-specific amino acid encodings concatenated with a zero-shot fitness prediction.
Training and hyperparameter tuning were performed using single mutant data (n=77) from the HSS (top four hot spots).
Traditional ISM campaigns were conducted in parallel for moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine to provide higher order mutants for model validation.
For metoclopramide, ISM yielded a quadruple mutant with nearly 30-fold improved activity over wild-type.
Model prediction performance was evaluated using NDCG and Spearman rank correlation; augmented models outperformed ridge regression alone.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk describes the ML-guided engineering approach, including the problem with cinchocaine ISM, the design of augmented ridge regression models, training on single mutant data, parallel ISM campaigns for benchmarking, and model evaluation.

events.summary: Analysis showed that using the full site saturation dataset provides more predictive power than reduced libraries.
Decided to use site saturation dataset and augmented EVmutation model with Georgiev encodings for strong predictive performance.
Experimentally tested top 25 predictions; ML model predicted variants enriched in high activity, some surpassing ISM quadruple mutants.
Best predicted mutant for metoclopramide contained a mutation (A424S) that was superseded in HSS by A424T, indicating model found a superior mutant overlooked by ISM.
Best predicted variant for cinchocaine had higher activity than best single mutation and contained a mutation (A205L) that decreased activity in HSS, showing model can avoid path dependencies.
Rank of experimentally tested predictions correlates poorly with predicted rank, indicating models may not accurately capture entire sequence-fitness landscape.
Applied ML-guided framework to six additional pharmaceutical compounds; best predicted mutant always outperformed best rational design.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers model evaluation, selection of final model, in silico screening, experimental validation of predictions, and application to additional compounds.

events.summary: Selected four hot spots and trained ML model to predict higher order mutants with increased activity for each reaction.
Tested top 24 predictions for each reaction and compared best variant to wt-McbA activity.
Observed yield increases from 1.6-fold to 34-fold over wt-McbA for six compounds; best predicted mutant always outperformed best rational design.
Some mutants gave only modest improvements, possibly due to low signal-to-noise in HSS leading to flat fitness landscapes difficult to model.
Engineered enzyme variant for troxipide accepted amine, reducing intermediate, and exhibited 34-fold increase in activity with maintained regioselectivity.
ML-predicted enzyme variants with 4 mutations had greater activity than combination of four best single mutants in all nine test cases, highlighting robustness.
Screening remains a bottleneck; LC-MS provided manageable solution (~3 min/sample) but may not suit all enzymes.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers ML-guided engineering for six additional pharmaceuticals, discussion of framework robustness, limitations, and methods overview.

events.summary: Comprehensive combinatorial double mutant McbA library generated by two successive rounds of saturation mutagenesis.
ML-predicted McbA variants ordered as gblocks and assembled into pJL1 backbone via Gibson assembly for CFPS.
Enzyme kinetics for moclobemide amine pair determined by coupling amide bond formation to NADH oxidation.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed methods for DNA library generation, protein expression, activity assays, and a specific experimental failure. Events focus on design and experimental steps with explicit quotes.

events.summary: Ridge regression models were augmented following Hsu et al. code, with variant featurization by concatenating zero-shot predictions and site-specific amino acid encodings.

Model evaluation and selection were performed retrospectively using moclobemide and metoclopramide data, training on single site saturation libraries (n≈80) and testing on withheld higher-order mutants (n≈200).
Hot spots were determined by selecting the four residues with highest improvement in yield among 64 tested.
Spearman correlation and NDCG were used to select the best zero-shot predictor and encoding strategy, with preference given to NDCG.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk details the machine learning methodology, including model design, hyperparameter tuning, evaluation, and computational efficiency, which are central to the paper's process.
This chunk contains only references, acknowledgements, author contributions, competing interests, and additional information. No research evolution events are present.

evidence_bank.quote: Enzyme engineering is limited by the challenge of rapidly generating and using large datasets of sequence-function relationships for predictive design.
screening strategies are generally low throughput, which constrains resampling mutations in iterative site saturation mutagenesis campaigns and can miss epistatic interactions
selection methods for directed evolution focus on “winning” enzymes for a single transformation, which limits the ability to collect positive and negative sequence-function relationships

we develop a machine learning (ML)-guided platform that integrates cell-free DNA assembly, cell-free gene expression, and functional assays to rapidly map fitness landscapes across protein sequence space and optimize enzymes for multiple, distinct chemical reactions.
We apply this platform to engineer amide synthetases by evaluating substrate preference for 1217 enzyme variants in 10,953 unique reactions.
Over these nine compounds, ML-predicted enzyme variants demonstrate 1.6- to 42-fold improved activity relative to the parent.
The campaign for cinchocaine was more difficult to navigate and we failed to observe beneficial mutations beyond a double mutant, despite taking multiple ISM paths (Fig. S14).
This result (i.e., running into dead ends during ISM) supported the need to include ML models in our framework that might capture epistatic interactions.
The key idea was to use single mutant data from the HSS to extrapolate higher order mutants with increased activity (Fig. 3A).

To achieve this goal, we chose to fit augmented ridge regression models with our data—as such models are simple and have been previously shown to outperform more sophisticated models for protein engineering
McbA variant feature representations consisted of site-specific amino acid encodings concatenated with a zero-shot fitness prediction
Training and hyperparameter tuning were performed using single mutant data (n=77) from the HSS (top four hot spots; Fig. 3B, C).
In parallel, we conducted a more traditional directed evolution campaign on each amide product (moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine) via iterative saturation mutagenesis (ISM).
This would provide valuable higher order mutations to validate and benchmark model performance given our objective of extrapolating from single to higher order mutations.
Three rounds of ISM for metoclopramide yielded a quadruple mutant that displayed nearly 30-fold improved activity over wt-McbA (Fig. S13).
The augmented models outperformed the ridge regression model alone when evaluated with NDCG.
this analysis indicated that utilizing all the data gathered from the site saturation dataset provides more predictive power
we decided to use the site saturation dataset and the augmented EVmutation model with Georgiev encodings given the strong predictive performance

the augmented ML model was able to predict McbA variants enriched in high activity for each amide product when tested experimentally, some even surpassing qm-McbA from both moclobemide and metoclopramide ISM campaigns

the best predicted mutant for metoclopramide contained a mutation (A424S) that was superseded in the HSS by a more active mutation (A424T) carried forward in ISM, indicating the model found a superior mutant that would have been overlooked using ISM alone

The best predicted variant for cinchocaine had significantly higher activity than the best single mutation on its own and surprisingly contained a mutation (A205L) that decreased activity compared to wt-McbA in the HSS

the rank of experimentally tested predictions correlates poorly with predicted rank (average Spearman's rank correlation of 0.21 ± 0.15), indicating the models may not accurately capture the entire sequence-fitness landscape
For each compound, the best predicted mutant always outperformed the best rational design
select four hot spots, and train our ML model to predict higher order mutants with increased activity
For each reaction, the top 24 predictions were tested, and the best variant was expressed, purified, and compared to wt-McbA activity.
We observed increases in yields ranging from 1.6-fold to 34-fold over wt-McbA for the six compounds we tested
the best predicted mutant always outperformed the best rational design

Some mutants gave only modest improvements, which may be an artifact of low signal-to-noise in the hot spot screens for some of the target compounds that were only detectable by MS. This can lead to flat fitness landscapes that are more difficult to model.
the quadruple mutant for troxipide that exhibits a 34-fold increase in activity without any sacrifice in specificity
in each of the nine test cases, ML-predicted enzyme variants with 4 mutations had greater activity than the combination of the four most active single-residue mutants alone
experimental screening methods for biocatalytic reactions remain a bottleneck
LC-MS provided a manageable solution, as has been found in other examples
The comprehensive combinatorial double mutant McbA library (used in Fig. S7e) was generated by two successive rounds of saturation mutagenesis with no particular residue targeted first.
All ML-predicted McbA variants were ordered as gblocks from IDT containing pJL1 5' and 3' Gibson assembly overhangs.

McbA apparent kinetics for the amine pair of moclobemide (4-(2-aminoethyl)morpholine) were determined by enzymatically coupling amide bond formation (and the concomitant release of AMP from the acyl-AMP intermediate by its substitution with the amine) with the oxidation of NADH (Fig. S9).
Ridge regression models were augmented following the code accompanying the elegant work of Hsu et al.
McbA variant sequence featurization was performed by concatenating zero-shot predictions with site-specific amino acid encodings.
Model evaluation and selection were first performed retrospectively by using the assay-labeled datasets from our moclobemide and metoclopramide engineering campaigns.
The four residues (hot spots) were determined by selecting the four residues that contained mutations with highest improvement in yield among the 64 residues tested.
Spearman correlation coefficient and NDCG were used to select the best zero-shot predictor and encoding strategy, with a preference given to NDCG.

The amidation assay was initiated by adding 3 $\mu$ L of crude CFPS reaction containing an expressed McbA variant, with final concentrations of 25 mM ATP, 25 mM acid, 25 mM amine, 10% v/v DMSO, and \~1 $\mu$ M of enzyme (determined by ${}^{14}$ C-leucine incorporation using previously described protocols $^{17}$ ).

muGFP was codon-optimized for E. coli and cloned into the pJL1 plasmid without a purification tag $^{38}$ . The cell-free DNA library generation was performed as follows: (1) the first PCR was performed in a 10- $\mu$ L reaction with 1 ng of plasmid template added, (2) 1 $\mu$ L of DpnI was added and incubated at 37 ${}^{\circ}$ C for 2 h, (3) the PCR was diluted 1:4 by the addition of 29 $\mu$ L of nuclease-free (NF) water, (4) 1 $\mu$ L of diluted DNA was added to a 3- $\mu$ L Gibson assembly reaction (self-made

Through efforts to build ML models and all ISM rounds, we explored the sequence-function landscape of McbA by assessing 2856 variants of McbA (1217 variants of which were for ML models), 1100 possible amide products, and 12,584 substrate pair-mutant reactions.

This can likely be attributed to the nature of the rich datasets mostly containing mutants with non-zero activity (64/77 for moclobemide and 62/77 for metoclopramide), preventing “holes” in training sets $^{14}$ .

We withheld variants in the training set to reflect common protein engineering strategies that do not exhaustively search the sequence space, including reduced codon libraries (NDT $^{50}$ and NRT $^{51}$ ), single amino acid scans $^{52}$ (here, we combine the commonly used glycine, alanine, proline, and cysteine scans), and reduced alphabets that naturally group amino acids by physiochemical properties (BLOSUM $^{53}$ ).

We tested three specific fitness predictors: the Evolutionary Scale Modeling (ESM)-1b transformer $^{46}$ trained on the UniRef50 database (universal), an EVmutation (EC) $^{47}$ probability density model trained on an MSA of evolutionarily related sequences (evolutionary), and MAESTRO $^{48}$ to estimate structure-based changes in unfolding free energy (structural).

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Enzyme engineering is limited by the challenge of rapidly generating and using large datasets of sequence-function relationships for predictive design.

canonical_events.summary: Enzyme engineering is limited by low-throughput directed evolution that misses epistatic interactions and focuses on single transformations.

A high-throughput, ML-guided platform integrating cell-free DNA assembly, cell-free gene expression, and functional assays can rapidly map fitness landscapes and optimize enzymes for multiple reactions.
Explore amidation reaction space of wild-type McbA by evaluating enzymatic substrate promiscuity with 1100 unique reactions to identify target reactions.
Implement cell-free protein synthesis workflow to rapidly generate sequence-defined protein libraries for McbA, avoiding laborious cloning steps.
Conduct hot spot screen (HSS) of 1216 single mutants across 64 residues for three target reactions (moclobemide, metoclopramide, cinchocaine) to identify beneficial mutations.
Run traditional iterative saturation mutagenesis (ISM) campaigns for moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine to generate higher-order mutants for benchmarking.
Cinchocaine ISM campaign fails to find beneficial mutations beyond a double mutant, motivating the need for ML to capture epistatic interactions.

Design augmented ridge regression ML models using single mutant data from HSS to predict higher-order mutants, with variant features concatenating site-specific amino acid encodings and zero-shot fitness predictions.
Train and evaluate ML models retrospectively on moclobemide and metoclopramide data, using NDCG and Spearman correlation to select best zero-shot predictor (EVmutation) and encoding (Georgiev).
Full site saturation dataset (n=77) provides more predictive power than reduced libraries; augmented models outperform ridge regression alone.
Experimentally test top 25 ML predictions for moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine; ML-predicted variants enriched in high activity, some surpassing ISM quadruple mutants.
ML model identifies superior mutants overlooked by ISM (e.g., A424S for metoclopramide) and avoids path dependencies (e.g., A205L for cinchocaine).
Apply ML-guided framework to six additional pharmaceutical compounds; best predicted mutant always outperforms best rational design, with 1.6- to 34-fold improvement.
Screening remains a bottleneck; LC-MS provides manageable solution (~3 min/sample) but may not suit all enzymes. Some mutants show modest improvements due to low signal-to-noise in HSS.

edges.reason: The problem motivates the hypothesis of using an ML-guided cell-free platform.
The hypothesis leads to exploring the reaction space of McbA to identify targets.
After identifying target reactions, a cell-free workflow is designed to generate mutant libraries.
The cell-free workflow enables the hot spot screen of single mutants.
In parallel to HSS, traditional ISM campaigns are run for benchmarking.
ISM fails for cinchocaine, highlighting a limitation of traditional methods.
The failure motivates the design of ML models to capture epistatic interactions.
HSS data is used to train the ML models.
Model design is followed by training and evaluation to select the best configuration.
Evaluation shows that full site saturation data and augmented models are superior.
The selected model is used to predict and test top variants.
Testing reveals that ML predictions can outperform ISM and avoid path dependencies.
The success on initial targets leads to application on six additional compounds.
The broader application reveals limitations such as screening bottlenecks and low signal-to-noise issues.

must_cover_points: Problem of low-throughput directed evolution missing epistatic interactions.
Hypothesis of ML-guided cell-free platform for rapid fitness landscape mapping.
Exploration of McbA substrate promiscuity to identify target reactions.
Implementation of cell-free DNA assembly and expression for mutant library generation.
Hot spot screen of 1216 single mutants for three initial targets.
Parallel ISM campaigns for benchmarking, including cinchocaine failure.
Design of augmented ridge regression models with zero-shot predictions.
Model training and evaluation using NDCG/Spearman, selecting EVmutation+Georgiev.
Experimental validation of top 25 predictions, showing ML variants surpass ISM.
Application to six additional compounds, demonstrating generalization.
Limitations: screening bottleneck, low signal-to-noise in HSS.
